refined_fr.refined_fr: CR-NGHE-005.1: The NGHE solution shall derive performance requirements from worksite mission context.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates three distinct obligations: (1) deriving requirements from mission context, (2) recording them, and (3) managing them over time. Atomicity requires separating these concerns. CR-NGHE-005.1 focuses on the derivation activity—the logical transformation from mission inputs to performance targets—which is solution-neutral and testable. Recording and management are separate concerns addressed in sub-FRs. The FR remains in the problem domain (what performance targets must be determined and from what inputs) without prescribing how derivation is implemented (algorithm, tool, process).
refined_fr.verification_idea: Demonstrate that given a defined worksite mission context (e.g., site layout, production goals, safety constraints), the system produces a documented set of performance targets (cycle time, energy efficiency, availability) that are traceable to mission inputs and satisfy acceptance criteria.
sub_frs: CR-NGHE-005.1: The NGHE solution shall derive performance requirements from worksite mission context.
CR-NGHE-005.2: The NGHE solution shall record derived performance requirements with traceability to worksite mission inputs.
CR-NGHE-005.3: The NGHE solution shall maintain a history of performance requirement baselines and calibration updates.
CR-NGHE-005.4: The NGHE solution shall manage performance requirements in accordance with worksite safety plan and operational policies.

candidate_dps: DP-NGHE-005.1a: Establish a parameterized mission-context intake process that captures worksite operational profile (hazard class, duty cycle, environmental conditions, safety constraints) and maps each input to corresponding performance target dimensions (accuracy, response time, reliability thresholds) via a documented traceability matrix.

DP-NGHE-005.1b: Implement a baseline performance specification repository indexed by worksite mission class, with automated lookup and instantiation of performance targets based on worksite safety plan classification and operational policy constraints, coupled to a versioned audit log of all baseline selections and calibration updates.

DP-NGHE-005.1c: Design a performance requirement derivation engine that accepts worksite mission context as structured input, applies a rule-based or model-driven transformation (e.g., decision tables, parametric equations) to generate performance targets, and produces a traceable output document linking each target to its source constraint.

DP-NGHE-005.1d: Establish a two-stage process: (1) worksite mission profiling module that extracts and normalizes safety plan and operational policy data into a canonical context model, and (2) a performance synthesis module that applies domain-specific heuristics or standards-based mappings to derive performance targets, with full traceability logging.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-NGHE-005.1d: Establish a two-stage process: (1) worksite mission profiling module that extracts and normalizes safety plan and operational policy data into a canonical context model, and (2) a performance synthesis module that applies domain-specific heuristics or standards-based mappings to derive performance targets, with full traceability logging.

potential_sources: Risk: Tight coupling between mission profiling and performance synthesis if the canonical context model is not sufficiently abstract—mitigation: define context model as a stable, domain-agnostic schema (e.g., hazard taxonomy, duty-cycle enumerations) that decouples input normalization from output derivation logic.

Risk: Dependency on external standards or heuristics for performance mapping—mitigation: encapsulate mapping rules in a configurable, versioned rule set that can be updated independently of the profiling module.

Risk: Traceability logging could become a bottleneck if not designed for asynchronous, non-blocking capture—mitigation: use event-sourced or append-only audit log pattern to avoid coupling performance synthesis to logging latency.
coupling_check.notes: The two-stage decomposition supports independence: mission profiling is decoupled from performance synthesis logic, allowing each to evolve separately. Traceability is a cross-cutting concern best handled via a dedicated audit mechanism rather than embedded in either stage. This design avoids monolithic coupling and supports testability of each stage in isolation.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Worksite safety plan and operational policies are available in a structured or semi-structured format; risk: if inputs are unstructured (free text), mission profiling module will require manual interpretation or NLP preprocessing—recommend: establish a worksite data intake standard or template.

Assumption: Performance target dimensions (accuracy, response time, reliability) are well-defined and measurable for the NGHE domain; risk: if domain lacks consensus on target metrics, derivation rules will be ambiguous—recommend: validate performance taxonomy with domain experts and document mapping rationale.

Assumption: Baseline performance specifications and calibration history are maintained with sufficient granularity; risk: if historical data is sparse or inconsistent, lookup and versioning will be unreliable—recommend: establish a baseline governance process and data quality gates.

Assumption: Worksite mission context is stable or changes infrequently; risk: if context changes rapidly (e.g., hazard reclassification), performance targets may become stale—recommend: define a change-detection and re-derivation trigger mechanism.

Assumption: Traceability requirements can be satisfied by logging derivation steps; risk: if regulatory or safety audits require formal proof of correctness, logging alone may be insufficient—recommend: consider formal verification or certification workflows if high-assurance is mandated.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR-NGHE-005.1 (refined): The NGHE solution shall derive performance requirements from worksite mission context by (1) normalizing worksite safety plan and operational policy inputs into a canonical mission profile, (2) applying documented, versioned performance mapping rules to synthesize performance targets, and (3) maintaining a complete, auditable history of all baseline selections, derivation decisions, and calibration updates. Acceptance criteria: (a) each performance target is traceable to at least one worksite input via a documented mapping rule, (b) all baseline and calibration changes are logged with timestamp, actor, and rationale, (c) the system supports re-derivation of targets upon worksite context change, and (d) traceability artifacts are exportable in a format suitable for safety audits.

im_boundary.name: Safety Services
im_boundary.scope: Centralized module responsible for safety plan profiling, operational policy normalization, performance target synthesis, and traceability logging across all vehicle and teleoperation contexts.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Receives worksite operational data, safety plan artifacts, and policy documents for profiling and normalization.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Outputs derived performance targets and safety constraints to guide vehicle and teleoperation task execution.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Emits traceability logs, compliance records, and safety deviation alerts based on performance synthesis results.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Communicates profiling requests and performance target updates to distributed vehicle and teleoperation nodes; receives acknowledgments and context updates.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit. Both worksite mission profiling and performance synthesis are inherently safety-domain functions. Traceability logging aligns with Safety Services' audit and compliance mandate. Minor diffusion: performance targets must be communicated to Vehicle and Teleoperation via Tasking interface, but logic remains contained.

Overly broad. NGHE Solution is the top-level system container. Placing DP here would blur boundaries between orchestration and domain-specific safety logic. Profiling and synthesis would diffuse across multiple sub-modules (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services), reducing cohesion and traceability.

Poor fit. Vehicle module is responsible for vehicle-specific control and state management, not cross-domain safety policy synthesis. Worksite mission profiling and performance target derivation are not vehicle-centric; they apply to the entire operational context. High diffusion: logic would leak into Teleoperation and Safety Services.

Poor fit. Teleoperation is responsible for remote operator interface and command relay, not safety policy normalization or performance synthesis. Profiling and heuristic application are orthogonal to teleoperation concerns. Significant diffusion: would require tight coupling to Safety Services and Vehicle for context and target application.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Safety Services is the natural owner of DP-NGHE-005.1d because: (1) worksite mission profiling and safety plan normalization are inherently safety-domain activities; (2) performance synthesis via domain-specific heuristics and standards-based mappings is a safety compliance function; (3) traceability logging is a core Safety Services responsibility for audit and regulatory compliance; (4) the module already owns safety constraint derivation and alert generation, making this DP a logical extension of existing cohesion.
containment_risks: Risk: Performance targets derived by Safety Services must be communicated to Vehicle and Teleoperation modules via Tasking interface. If target application logic is not clearly separated from target derivation, coupling may increase.

Risk: Worksite mission profiling requires ingestion of diverse operational policy formats. If normalization logic becomes too specialized or context-dependent, it may diffuse into domain-specific sub-modules.
Risk: Traceability logging at scale may create performance bottlenecks if not properly decoupled from real-time safety alert generation.
Risk: If Vehicle or Teleoperation modules attempt to override or locally synthesize performance targets, traceability and compliance guarantees are compromised.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: HIGH. Acceptance criteria explicitly require documented performance targets traceable to worksite inputs and maintained history of baselines/calibration updates. Safety Services module boundary provides clear ownership for traceability logging and compliance record generation. Verification can be grounded in: (1) audit trail of profiling inputs → synthesis logic → target outputs, (2) version control of baseline performance targets, (3) cross-reference of derived targets to source safety plan artifacts and operational policies.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: CLEAR. FR-NGHE-005.1 (derive performance requirements from worksite mission context) maps unambiguously to Safety Services IM boundary. The two-stage process (profiling + synthesis) is fully contained within Safety Services scope. External interfaces (Telemetry in, Tasking/SafetyAlert out, Network Link bidirectional) establish clean contract boundaries for consuming worksite context and distributing derived targets without diffusing synthesis logic.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-NGHE-005.1d correctly operationalizes FR-NGHE-005.1. The two-stage decomposition (worksite mission profiling module + performance synthesis module) is logically sound and addresses the functional requirement with explicit mechanisms: (1) canonical context model normalizes heterogeneous safety plan and policy inputs, (2) domain-specific heuristics and standards-based mappings provide repeatable derivation logic, (3) full traceability logging satisfies compliance and auditability concerns. DP is sufficiently detailed to guide IM allocation and testable against acceptance criteria.

diffusion_risks: MODERATE: Performance target application logic must remain strictly separated from target derivation logic. If Vehicle or Teleoperation modules begin to locally adjust or override targets derived by Safety Services, traceability chain breaks and compliance guarantees are compromised. Mitigation: enforce read-only consumption of Tasking interface outputs; any target modification must flow back through Safety Services for re-synthesis and re-logging.

MODERATE: Worksite mission profiling requires ingestion and normalization of diverse operational policy formats (e.g., site-specific safety plans, regulatory standards, equipment constraints). If normalization heuristics become overly specialized or context-dependent, logic may diffuse into Vehicle or Teleoperation sub-modules. Mitigation: establish a formal policy schema and normalization library within Safety Services; document all format-specific mappings as canonical transformations.

LOW-MODERATE: Traceability logging at scale (continuous profiling updates, real-time performance synthesis, audit trail persistence) may create performance bottlenecks if not decoupled from real-time safety alert generation. Mitigation: implement asynchronous logging pipeline; separate hot-path alert generation from cold-path compliance record writing.

LOW: Network Link interface (bidirectional communication with distributed Vehicle and Teleoperation nodes) introduces potential for context drift if nodes cache stale performance targets. Mitigation: implement explicit versioning and acknowledgment protocol for target distribution; Safety Services must track which nodes have accepted which target versions.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: EXCELLENT CONTAINMENT (0.92 score justified). Safety Services is the natural and cohesive owner of DP-NGHE-005.1d. Worksite mission profiling and performance synthesis are inherently safety-domain functions; they do not belong in Vehicle (vehicle-centric control) or Teleoperation (operator interface relay). The module already owns safety constraint derivation and alert generation, making this DP a logical extension of existing responsibilities. Minor diffusion risk is limited to the Tasking interface boundary (target communication), which is a necessary and well-defined contract. No evidence of logic leakage into Vehicle, Teleoperation, or NGHE Solution top-level orchestration.

recommended_revisions: REVISION 1 (DP Clarity): Explicitly separate 'performance target derivation' from 'performance target application' in DP-NGHE-005.1d. Add a third sub-stage: (3) target distribution and versioning module that manages Tasking interface communication and tracks acknowledgments from Vehicle and Teleoperation nodes. This clarifies that Safety Services owns derivation and distribution, but not application logic.

REVISION 2 (IM Interface Contract): Formalize the Tasking interface contract to include: (a) target payload schema (performance metric name, value, unit, derivation timestamp, source policy artifact), (b) version identifier and immutability guarantee, (c) read-only consumption semantics (Vehicle and Teleoperation must not modify targets locally), (d) exception handling (what happens if a node cannot meet a derived target). Document in Safety Services module specification.

REVISION 3 (Traceability Acceptance Criteria): Extend acceptance criteria to require: (a) end-to-end audit trail linking each performance target to its source worksite safety plan and operational policy inputs, (b) timestamped baseline history with change justification, (c) compliance record linking each target to applicable regulatory standards or domain-specific heuristics applied. Make this a formal Safety Services output artifact.

REVISION 4 (Diffusion Guard): Add a design rule to the Safety Services module specification: 'No performance target synthesis logic shall be implemented outside Safety Services. Vehicle and Teleoperation modules shall consume targets via Tasking interface only. Any local performance adjustment or override must be logged as a deviation and escalated to Safety Services for re-synthesis.' This prevents creeping diffusion.

REVISION 5 (Policy Normalization Library): Establish a formal, versioned policy normalization library within Safety Services that documents all supported safety plan formats, operational policy schemas, and their canonical mappings. This prevents ad-hoc normalization logic from diffusing into sub-modules and ensures consistency across worksite contexts.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-IM, FR-DP, DP-IM) pass with high confidence, and containment is excellent (0.92). However, the recommended revisions expose critical clarity gaps in DP-NGHE-005.1d that must be resolved before proceeding to sub-FR decomposition. Specifically: (1) the DP conflates 'derivation' and 'distribution' logic without explicit separation, creating ambiguity about Safety Services ownership boundaries; (2) the Tasking interface contract lacks formal specification of immutability, versioning, and read-only semantics, which are essential for preventing diffusion into Vehicle and Teleoperation modules; (3) acceptance criteria do not explicitly require end-to-end traceability artifacts linking targets to source policies and regulatory standards. Given high safety criticality, these gaps must be closed at the current level before decomposing into sub-FRs. Revising now prevents rework downstream and hardens the design against diffusion risks flagged in the gate analysis.

revise.why: DP-NGHE-005.1d must be clarified to explicitly separate three distinct sub-stages: (1) worksite mission profiling (ingest and normalize safety plans/policies into canonical context model), (2) performance synthesis (apply domain heuristics and standards-based mappings to derive targets), (3) target distribution and versioning (manage Tasking interface communication, track node acknowledgments, enforce immutability). This separation makes ownership boundaries unambiguous and prevents logic diffusion. Additionally, the DP must explicitly state that Vehicle and Teleoperation modules consume targets via read-only Tasking interface only; any local adjustment or override is prohibited and must be escalated to Safety Services for re-synthesis. This is a critical safety design rule that must be embedded in the DP logic, not left to implementation discretion.

assumptions: Safety Services module has sufficient computational and I/O capacity to perform real-time worksite mission profiling and performance synthesis without introducing latency that degrades Vehicle or Teleoperation responsiveness.
Worksite safety plans and operational policies can be normalized into a canonical context model with acceptable fidelity loss; no critical safety semantics are lost in normalization.
Vehicle and Teleoperation modules can be contractually bound to consume performance targets as read-only; no architectural or operational constraint prevents this enforcement.
Network Link interface supports versioning and acknowledgment protocols required for tracking which nodes have accepted which target versions.
Regulatory and domain-specific standards applicable to worksite mission profiling are stable and well-documented; heuristic mappings can be formalized and maintained.
open_risks: MODERATE: If worksite safety plan formats are highly heterogeneous or poorly documented, normalization heuristics may become overly specialized and diffuse into Vehicle or Teleoperation sub-modules. Mitigation: establish formal policy schema and versioned normalization library within Safety Services before IM allocation.

MODERATE: Performance target application logic in Vehicle and Teleoperation modules may drift toward local adjustment or override if Tasking interface contract is not formally enforced. Mitigation: implement read-only consumption semantics and escalation protocol; add design rule to Safety Services module specification prohibiting local target modification.

MODERATE: Traceability logging at scale (continuous profiling updates, real-time synthesis, audit trail persistence) may create performance bottlenecks or storage overhead. Mitigation: implement asynchronous logging pipeline; separate hot-path alert generation from cold-path compliance record writing.

LOW-MODERATE: Network Link interface may introduce context drift if Vehicle or Teleoperation nodes cache stale performance targets. Mitigation: implement explicit versioning and acknowledgment protocol; Safety Services must track target version acceptance across all nodes.
LOW: Acceptance criteria do not yet explicitly require end-to-end traceability artifacts; this must be formalized before verification planning.
evidence_needed: Formal specification of Tasking interface contract, including: (a) target payload schema (metric name, value, unit, derivation timestamp, source policy artifact, version ID), (b) immutability guarantee and read-only consumption semantics, (c) exception handling protocol for nodes unable to meet derived targets, (d) acknowledgment and versioning mechanism.

Documented policy normalization library within Safety Services, including: (a) supported safety plan formats and operational policy schemas, (b) canonical mappings for each format, (c) version control and change justification for all mappings.

Design rule for Safety Services module specification: 'No performance target synthesis logic shall be implemented outside Safety Services. Vehicle and Teleoperation modules shall consume targets via Tasking interface only. Any local performance adjustment or override must be logged as a deviation and escalated to Safety Services for re-synthesis.'

Extended acceptance criteria for FR-NGHE-005.1 requiring: (a) end-to-end audit trail linking each performance target to source worksite safety plan and operational policy inputs, (b) timestamped baseline history with change justification, (c) compliance record linking each target to applicable regulatory standards or domain-specific heuristics applied.

Architectural analysis of traceability logging performance impact; confirmation that asynchronous logging pipeline can support real-time synthesis without introducing unacceptable latency or storage overhead.
Network Link interface specification confirming support for versioning and acknowledgment protocols required for target distribution tracking.
